summary: Fresh pyfly re-analysis: hexagonal cleanliness, data/persistence adapters, decorator/DX parity, remaining gaps

findings.what: The 'query compiler' is not a port — SQL rendering is baked into the core DSL and hardcoded to PostgreSQL. pyfly factors compilation behind QueryMethodCompilerPort.compile(parsed, entity) so each adapter (SQLAlchemy, MongoDB, DynamoDB) lowers a backend-neutral ParsedQuery/Specification its own way. In Rust, Filter::to_sql and Specification::to_sql ARE the rendering, and they emit Postgres-only syntax: `$1` numbered placeholders (MySQL/SQLite want `?`), double-quoted identifiers (MySQL uses backticks unless ANSI_QUOTES), `= ANY($n)` for IN (MySQL/SQLite have no array param — need expanded `IN (?, ?, ...)`), and `ILIKE` (MySQL/SQLite have no ILIKE — need LOWER() or COLLATE). So a 'new DB' is NOT just a new adapter today: you'd be re-implementing rendering that lives in the shared crate, or shipping wrong SQL. This is the single biggest hexagonal-cleanliness gap for the persistence dimension.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/data/ports/compiler.py (QueryMethodCompilerPort.compile); relational/sqlalchemy/query_compiler.py (QueryMethodCompiler builds SQLAlchemy expressions, dialect-neutral); document/mongodb/query_compiler.py (MongoQueryMethodCompiler builds $-operator dicts). vs Rust crates/data/src/filter.rs:215-267 (to_sql) and specification.rs:225-294 (render_predicate) — Postgres syntax inline.
findings.rust_state: firefly-data/src/filter.rs and specification.rs render Postgres SQL directly; query_parser.rs only lowers to Filter/Specification or evaluates in-memory (no compile-to-backend). The only real DB adapter is PostgresReactiveRepository in reactive.rs, hard-bound to tokio-postgres. No sqlx anywhere in the workspace (grep confirms only starter-data README mentions it aspirationally).
findings.recommendation: Introduce a Dialect abstraction in firefly-data so the core DSL stays storage-agnostic. Add `pub trait SqlDialect { fn placeholder(&self, n: usize) -> String; fn quote_ident(&self, id: &str) -> String; fn render_in(&self, field, n_args) -> String; fn ilike(&self, field, ph) -> String; }` with Postgres/MySql/Sqlite impls, and change Filter/Specification to expose `to_sql_with(&dyn SqlDialect)` (keep to_sql() as the Postgres default for back-compat). Then firefly-data-sqlx is a thin adapter selecting the dialect at runtime from the sqlx::Any backend kind. This is the precondition that makes 'new DB = new adapter' actually true.

findings.what: No firefly-data-sqlx crate exists. There is no generic relational adapter implementing Repository/ReactiveCrudRepository/ReactiveSpecificationRepository over sqlx for pg/mysql/sqlite. pyfly's SQLAlchemy adapter is one codebase serving all three relational backends; Rust has only a Postgres-specific tokio-postgres repo with a hand-written inserter closure per entity.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/data/relational/sqlalchemy/repository.py (generic Repository[T,ID]: save/find_by_id/find_all/find_paginated/find_all_by_spec/find_all_by_spec_paged/count/exists/save_all/find_all_by_ids/delete_all/delete_all_entities) backed by AsyncSession across pg/mysql/sqlite.
findings.rust_state: crates/data/src/reactive.rs PostgresReactiveRepository<T,ID> over tokio-postgres only; requires a per-entity inserter closure rendering INSERT..ON CONFLICT (Postgres-specific upsert). MemoryRepository.find does not honour predicates. No sqlx, no MySQL/SQLite path, no spec/pageable execution against a real relational DB.
findings.recommendation: Create crates/data-sqlx (firefly-data-sqlx) using sqlx with the `any` driver (or feature-gated postgres/mysql/sqlite). Provide SqlxRepository<T,ID> and SqlxReactiveRepository<T,ID> that take a TableConfig + RowMapper (FromRow) and implement the existing firefly-data traits, compiling Filter/Specification/Pageable via the new SqlDialect. Upsert must be dialect-aware (ON CONFLICT for pg/sqlite, ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE for mysql). Stream reads via sqlx::query::fetch (returns a Stream) into a Flux to preserve the lazy/backpressured contract. Real-infra test against pg+mysql+sqlite (env-gated like the existing postgres_reactive_round_trip).

findings.what: No firefly-data-mongodb crate exists and the ports are not proven to fit a document store. pyfly puts MongoSpecification (-> $and/$or/$nor filter dicts) and MongoRepository behind the SAME Repository[T,ID] surface as relational. Rust's Specification only knows how to render SQL or evaluate in-memory — there is no `to_mongo_filter()` lowering, so a Mongo adapter cannot reuse the Specification tree without re-walking it externally.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/data/document/mongodb/{repository.py (MongoRepository, Beanie), specification.py (MongoSpecification &/|/~ -> $and/$or/$nor), query_compiler.py (MongoQueryMethodCompiler: ParsedQuery -> pymongo filter dict + sort), document.py (BaseDocument audit fields)}.
findings.rust_state: crates/data/src/specification.rs exposes to_sql / to_filter / matches but NO document/BSON lowering. query_parser.rs lowers only to Filter/Specification. No mongodb driver dependency, no document repository.
findings.recommendation: Two-part: (a) add Specification::to_mongo() -> serde_json::Value (a $-operator filter doc) and ParsedQuery::to_mongo() in firefly-data so the document lowering lives next to the SQL/in-memory ones (keeps the tree the single source of truth, like pyfly factoring it behind the spec). (b) Create crates/data-mongodb (firefly-data-mongodb) using the official `mongodb` crate: MongoRepository<T,ID: Serialize+DeserializeOwned> implementing Repository/ReactiveCrudRepository, with find_by_spec(_paged) consuming Specification::to_mongo, sort from Pageable, and a BaseDocument-equivalent audit-stamps mixin. Real-infra test against a mongod container.

findings.what: Auditing and soft-delete have the pure primitives but no repository integration that auto-applies them, so each adapter would re-thread the guard by hand. pyfly's SoftDeleteRepository overrides EVERY read path (find_by_id/find_all/find_paginated/find_all_by_ids/find_all_by_spec/find_all_by_spec_paged/count) to inject `deleted_at IS NULL`, and AuditingEntityListener auto-stamps on every insert/update via ORM events. Rust has SoftDeletePolicy.apply()/apply_spec() and Auditor.on_insert/on_update as free functions, but nothing calls them automatically inside a repository.
findings.pyfly_ref: relational/sqlalchemy/soft_delete.py (SoftDeleteRepository overrides all readers + delete/restore/hard_delete), auditing.py (AuditingEntityListener before_insert/before_update). Rust crates/data/src/soft_delete.rs (SoftDeletePolicy, manual apply), auditing.rs (Auditor, manual on_insert/on_update).
findings.rust_state: Primitives exist and are well-tested but are opt-in helpers. PostgresReactiveRepository does NOT apply soft-delete or auditing. find_all/find_by_spec leak deleted rows; save does not stamp audit fields.
findings.recommendation: Build the auto-application into the new sqlx/mongodb adapters: have the adapter hold an Option<SoftDeletePolicy> and Option<Auditor> and apply them in every read/write path (the SoftDeleteRepository behaviour). For the implicit current-user (pyfly resolves it from RequestContext), wire firefly-data adapters to read the user from the existing request-context / security crate via a small UserProvider closure injected at construction, so on_insert/on_update get the user without the caller passing it each time. Optionally a #[derive(Auditable, SoftDeletable)] macro (ties into the planned firefly-macros milestone) to mark the columns.

findings.what: The transactional story does not connect to async sqlx. pyfly has a unified backend-neutral @transactional that dispatches to a relational runner (full Propagation/Isolation/read_only/rollback_for semantics, repository session-patching, replica routing) or a mongo runner. Rust's firefly-transactional is a SYNCHRONOUS Go-style port over a blocking Database/Executor/Transaction port with SqlValue rows — it cannot host an async sqlx pool, and firefly-data adapters cannot enlist in it.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/data/transactional.py (unified @transactional, Propagation, Isolation), relational/sqlalchemy/transactional.py (run_relational_transaction: REQUIRED/REQUIRES_NEW/MANDATORY/NEVER/NOT_SUPPORTED/SUPPORTS, isolation, read_only->replica, rollback_for/no_rollback_for), document/mongodb/transactional.py (run_mongo_transaction).
findings.rust_state: crates/transactional/src/lib.rs is sync (Executor/Database/Transaction traits, SqlValue, with_tx) — modelled on Go database/sql. No async variant, no sqlx::Transaction integration, no propagation/isolation enums, no read-only->replica tie-in to RoutingPolicy.
findings.recommendation: Add an async transactional surface for the relational adapter: either an `async fn with_tx` in firefly-data-sqlx that begins a sqlx::Transaction, exposes propagation (at least REQUIRED reuse + REQUIRES_NEW) and isolation, sets read_only() scope so RoutingPolicy routes to the replica, and commits/rolls-back per rollback_for/no_rollback_for. Mongo gets an analogous with_session/with_transaction. Keep the existing sync firefly-transactional for the Go-parity blocking path; the async one is the sqlx/mongo home. Do not try to overload the sync port with async.

findings.what: Data-layer health and query metrics have no Rust home. pyfly ships a SELECT 1 HealthIndicator reporting the dialect, and a SqlAlchemyQueryMetrics adapter recording query duration histogram / count / error counters with a bounded operation label. Rust firefly-data has neither, so a new adapter would have to invent its own observability wiring.
findings.pyfly_ref: relational/health.py (SqlAlchemyHealthIndicator -> HealthStatus UP/DOWN + dialect), relational/metrics.py (SqlAlchemyQueryMetrics: pyfly_db_query_duration_seconds / pyfly_db_queries_total / pyfly_db_query_errors_total labelled by SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/OTHER).
findings.rust_state: No DB health indicator or query-metrics in firefly-data; firefly-actuator has the HealthIndicator trait and firefly-observability has the metrics recorder, but nothing in the data layer plugs into them.
findings.recommendation: In firefly-data-sqlx, ship a SqlxHealthIndicator (implements firefly-actuator's HealthIndicator: runs SELECT 1, reports backend kind) and a query-metrics layer that wraps the pool/executor to record the same three metrics (duration histogram, count, errors) against firefly-observability's recorder, with the operation label normalised to the bounded set. Mirror metric names with a `firefly_db_*` prefix for cross-port consistency.

findings.what: Named datasources and read/write routing exist as generic registries but are not bound to a real relational pool, so multi-datasource and replica routing are theoretical until an adapter consumes them. pyfly's build_named_data_sources reads `pyfly.data.relational.datasources.<name>` config into engine+sessionmaker factories, and RoutingSessionFactory hands the replica session inside read_only().
findings.pyfly_ref: relational/named_datasources.py (build_named_data_sources from config), relational/routing.py (RoutingSessionFactory), relational/auto_configuration.py (wires engine/session/replica/named datasources/migrations/health/metrics from config keys).
findings.rust_state: crates/data/src/routing.rs RoutingPolicy<S>/NamedDataSources<S> are generic over the session/factory type S and fully tested, but no relational adapter instantiates them with sqlx pools, and there is no config-driven builder reading datasource URLs.
findings.recommendation: In firefly-data-sqlx, provide a config-driven builder (reading datasource URLs from firefly-config, e.g. firefly.data.relational.url + .replica + .datasources.<name>) that produces RoutingPolicy<PgPool/AnyPool> and NamedDataSources<Pool>. Wire it into firefly-starter-data so a service gets primary+replica+named pools and the read_only()->replica routing for free, matching pyfly's auto_configuration.

findings.what: Migrations are split from the data adapter and not auto-run. pyfly's MigrationRunner applies migrations on startup gated by `pyfly.data.relational.migrations.enabled`, reusing the app's datasource. Rust has a capable firefly-migrations crate (V{n}__desc.sql, checksums, idempotency) but it uses its own sync Database port and is not wired to start automatically off the relational adapter's pool.
findings.pyfly_ref: relational/migrations.py (MigrationRunner.start applies migrations at startup), relational/auto_configuration.py (gates it on config).
findings.rust_state: crates/migrations/src/lib.rs is a solid runner over a sync Database port + Source (Dir/Embedded/Slice). It is not connected to firefly-data or run on startup; the adapter and the migration runner speak different (sync vs async) DB ports.
findings.recommendation: Add a thin bridge so firefly-data-sqlx can run firefly-migrations against its sqlx pool on startup when a `migrations.enabled` flag is set (adapt the sqlx pool to the migrations Database port, or add an async run variant). This is lower priority because firefly-migrations already works standalone; the gap is only the auto-wire convenience pyfly provides.

findings.what: Optimistic locking (version column) and Query-by-Example have pyfly equivalents with no Rust counterpart. pyfly ships VersionedMixin (auto-incrementing version, StaleDataError on conflict) and FilterUtils.by/from_dict/from_example (QBE -> Specification). Neither is represented in firefly-data, so adapters lack a standard optimistic-concurrency and example-query surface.
findings.pyfly_ref: relational/sqlalchemy/entity.py (VersionedMixin, version_id_col), relational/sqlalchemy/filter.py (FilterOperator + FilterUtils QBE), document/mongodb/filter.py (Mongo QBE).
findings.rust_state: firefly-data has no version/optimistic-lock helper and no QBE builder. (eventsourcing has its own optimistic concurrency, but that is a different concern from CRUD-entity versioning.)
findings.recommendation: Add a small Versioned audit-style helper (version: i64) plus an UPDATE ... WHERE id=? AND version=? guard in the sqlx adapter returning a OptimisticLock error on 0 rows affected, and a FilterUtils-style by()/from_serializable() builder in firefly-data that turns a partial serde value into a conjunction Specification (eq per non-null field). Lower priority — convenience parity rather than a blocking hexagonal gap.

findings.what: No framework-provided assembly/wiring layer that maps config strings to concrete vendor adapters. pyfly's per-domain `auto_configuration.py` reads a config key AND constructs the adapter via lazy import (e.g. `NotificationsAutoConfiguration.email_provider` reads `pyfly.notifications.email.provider`, then `from pyfly.notifications.providers.sendgrid import SendGridEmailProvider; return SendGridEmailProvider(api_key=...)`). In Rust this is impossible inside the core crate (a core crate depending on its own adapter crates would invert/cycle the dependency), so the core crates stop at a *selection enum* and leave construction to the application author. The result: each app must hand-write the `match selection { SendGrid => firefly_notifications_sendgrid::SendGridEmailProvider::new(...) , ... }` switch itself (see samples/reactive-banking/src/main.rs lines 72-83 hand-rolling `match env::var("FIREFLY_TEST_KAFKA_BROKERS") => new_kafka_broker(...)`). pyfly users get full provider wiring for free from one config key; Rust users get a string-parsing enum and a TODO.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/notifications/auto_configuration.py (NotificationsAutoConfiguration.email_provider / sms_provider / push_provider); src/pyfly/eda/auto_configuration.py (EdaAutoConfiguration.event_publisher); src/pyfly/idp/auto_configuration.py; src/pyfly/ecm/auto_configuration.py
findings.rust_state: crates/notifications/src/config.rs ships EmailProviderSelection/SmsProviderSelection/PushProviderSelection enums whose docstrings say 'leave the actual construction of vendor adapters to the vendor crates'. crates/ecm/src/factory.rs::from_config builds only `local`/`noop` and returns EcmError::Provider('requires firefly-ecm-storage-aws — construct its Store and pass it in') for every cloud provider. No workspace crate depends on more than one adapter crate; grep for deps on adapter crates returns only the adapter crates themselves and the reactive-banking sample.
findings.recommendation: Add a feature-gated assembly facade crate (e.g. `firefly-adapters` or per-domain `firefly-notifications-autoconfig`) that depends on all vendor adapter crates behind cargo features (`sendgrid`, `twilio`, `kafka`, `redis`, `keycloak`, `s3`, ...) and exposes a real `build_email_provider(&Config) -> Arc<dyn EmailProvider>` / `build_broker(&EdaConfig) -> Arc<dyn Broker>` that does the full string→constructor wiring pyfly does — the variant whose feature is off returns a typed 'feature not enabled, add features=["sendgrid"]' error. This is the missing top of the hexagon: ports in core, adapters in leaf crates, and ONE composition crate that knows about both. Mirror it consistently for eda/cache/idp/ecm/notifications/config-server.

findings.what: The core `firefly-eda` crate carries STALE stub scaffolds (`kafka.rs`, `rabbitmq.rs`) that duplicate the names AND config types of the real adapter crates, which already exist and are shipped 'Full'. `firefly_eda::new_kafka_broker(KafkaConfig)` always returns `Err(EdaError::KafkaUnavailable)`, and `firefly_eda::KafkaConfig` (fields: brokers, client_id, consumer_group, tls, schema_reg_url) is a DIFFERENT, incompatible struct from the real `firefly_eda_kafka::KafkaConfig`. The doc comments are factually wrong: eda/src/kafka.rs says 'Until a real Kafka-backed crate is registered (planned: firefly-eda-kafka)' and the eda README says 'Status: Partial (Kafka/RabbitMQ scaffolds)' / 'Until those land' — but firefly-eda-kafka/-rabbitmq/-redis/-postgres are all shipped Full. This creates a two-API trap: a user who writes `use firefly_eda::{new_kafka_broker, KafkaConfig}` (as the eda README's own example at line 289 still does) gets a runtime KafkaUnavailable error instead of a real broker.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/eda/adapters/kafka.py + rabbitmq.py are the ONLY kafka/rabbitmq code in pyfly; there is no duplicate stub in the core eda package. pyfly's eda/__init__.py exports ports + memory only; transports are reached via adapters/.
findings.rust_state: crates/eda/src/kafka.rs + rabbitmq.rs (stub factories returning EdaError::KafkaUnavailable/RabbitMqUnavailable), exported from crates/eda/src/lib.rs lines 131/134; duplicated by the real crates/eda-kafka and crates/eda-rabbitmq. crates/eda/src/error.rs defines KafkaUnavailable/RabbitMqUnavailable but NO Redis/Postgres equivalents (asymmetric — eda-redis & eda-postgres exist with no matching core stub). eda/tests/eda_test.rs is the only non-eda-crate consumer of the stub factories.
findings.recommendation: Delete the kafka.rs/rabbitmq.rs stubs, the `new_kafka_broker`/`new_rabbitmq_broker`/`KafkaConfig`/`RabbitMqConfig` re-exports, and the `KafkaUnavailable`/`RabbitMqUnavailable` error variants from `firefly-eda`. The 'fail loud when a transport is selected but not wired' behavior belongs in the new assembly facade (finding 1), keyed off a cargo feature, not in the port crate. Update eda/README.md ('Status: Partial' → 'Full', drop the 'Until those land' paragraph and the misleading `use firefly_eda::{new_kafka_broker}` example) and crates/cache/src/lib.rs (drop 'once the Redis adapter ships in the next minor'). This removes the dual-`KafkaConfig` trap and the asymmetry with redis/postgres.

findings.what: The `idp` domain is the only OTHER-tech domain with NO config-selection helper at all — inconsistent with its siblings. notifications has provider-selection enums (config.rs), ecm has `from_config` (factory.rs), eda core has the (stale) stub factories, but idp/src/ has only lib.rs (port) + web.rs (router). pyfly's `idp/auto_configuration.py` selects internal-db | keycloak | cognito | azure-ad from `pyfly.idp.provider` and constructs the matching adapter with its per-provider config keys. A Rust user wiring IdP has zero framework guidance on the provider→crate mapping and must discover all four adapter crates and their constructor signatures unaided.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/idp/auto_configuration.py (IdpAutoConfiguration: provider key internal-db|keycloak|cognito|azure-ad, with per-provider config keys idp.keycloak.base-url / idp.cognito.user-pool-id / idp.azure.tenant-id etc.)
findings.rust_state: crates/idp/src/ contains only lib.rs and web.rs. No IdpProviderSelection enum, no from_config factory, no config struct. The four adapter crates (idp-internal-db, idp-keycloak, idp-azure-ad, idp-aws-cognito) each implement firefly_idp::Adapter correctly but there is no selection surface tying a config string to them.
findings.recommendation: At minimum add an `IdpProviderSelection` enum + config struct in crates/idp/src/config.rs mirroring crates/notifications/src/config.rs (parse internal-db|keycloak|cognito|azure-ad → variant), so idp is consistent with notifications/ecm. Better: fold the full construction into the assembly facade from finding 1 (`build_idp(&IdpConfig) -> Arc<dyn Adapter>` behind features). Document the four provider config key-sets to match pyfly's.

findings.what: No documented, end-to-end 'implement this port to add a new <tech>' tutorial. The port→adapter split is excellently catalogued in MODULES.md (sections '03 — Adapters' / 'Infrastructure adapters' map each adapter crate to `cache::Adapter → Redis`, `eda::Broker → Kafka`, etc.) and every port trait is well-documented, but there is no narrative walkthrough showing a reader how to write a NEW adapter crate (e.g. a GCS ECM store or a NATS broker): create crate, depend on firefly-<domain>, impl the trait, wire it. The Java/pyfly side has the `implement-hexagonal-adapter` skill; the Rust book has chapters 10 (eda) and 17 (caching) but no 'add your own adapter' chapter. Combined with the missing assembly crate (finding 1), the 'add a new tech' story is real at the trait level but undocumented and unsupported at the wiring level.
findings.pyfly_ref: fireflyframework-claude-skills implement-hexagonal-adapter skill (references real IDP/ECM/Notifications adapter examples); pyfly's per-domain adapters/ + auto_configuration.py serve as the worked example.
findings.rust_state: docs/book/src chapters cover using built-in adapters but none walks through authoring a new adapter crate against a port. MODULES.md lists the mapping but is a catalogue, not a how-to. The cleanest reference impls (cache-redis, eda-redis) exist but aren't cross-linked as 'copy this to add a backend'.
findings.recommendation: Add a book chapter (e.g. '21-writing-an-adapter') that walks through cloning crates/cache-redis as a template: new `firefly-cache-mybackend` crate, `impl firefly_cache::Adapter`, the byte-transparency contract, health_check, and how to register it through the new assembly facade. Reference it from each port crate's README ('To add a backend, see …').

findings.what: Minor inconsistency in the ecm `from_config` factory's failure mode vs pyfly. pyfly's EcmAutoConfiguration lazily imports and fully constructs s3/azure/docusign/adobe/logalty adapters; the Rust `from_config` returns `Err(EcmError::Provider("... requires firefly-ecm-storage-aws — construct its Store and pass it in"))` for every non-local/non-noop provider. This is a defensible consequence of the dependency-inversion constraint, but it means `from_config` silently can NEVER succeed for any production provider — it is effectively a local-dev-only convenience that masquerades as the 'analog of EcmAutoConfiguration'. The same limitation applies to notifications' selection enums (they parse but cannot construct).
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/ecm/auto_configuration.py (EcmAutoConfiguration.document_storage / esignature_adapter build the real cloud adapters)
findings.rust_state: crates/ecm/src/factory.rs::from_config (lines 136-179): only `local`/`filesystem` storage and `noop` esignature construct successfully; s3/aws/azure/docusign/adobe/logalty all return EcmError::Provider pointing the caller at the adapter crate.
findings.recommendation: Move the real cloud/vendor construction into the assembly facade (finding 1) so `from_config`-style entry points actually return working adapters when the corresponding cargo feature is on. Keep the core ecm `from_config` honest by either (a) restricting it to `local`/`noop` and renaming/doc'ing it as the local-only fast path, or (b) deleting it in favor of the facade. Same treatment for notifications' selection enums (pair each with a facade constructor).

findings.what: No firefly-macros crate exists. pyfly exposes ~21 ergonomic decorators; the Rust port has ALL the runtime machinery (Bus, Container, Scheduler, Saga, AspectRegistry, axum web, resilience structs) but ZERO proc-macros. Verified: no `proc-macro = true`, no syn/quote deps anywhere (grep over all crates/*/Cargo.toml). Services are wired with closures and builders (samples/orders/src/core.rs uses bus.register(move |req| async {...}); samples/reactive-banking/src/web.rs hand-writes axum routes; saga.rs uses Saga::new().step(Step::new(...).with_compensation(...))). The MIGRATION-GUIDE.md explicitly states the design is 'annotation -> builder/registration call'. This is the single biggest DX gap vs Spring-Boot-like ergonomics.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/{container/stereotypes.py,container/bean.py,cqrs/decorators.py,scheduling/decorators.py,cache/decorators.py,transactional/saga/annotations.py,aop/decorators.py,web/mappings.py,security/decorators.py,resilience/*}
findings.rust_state: crates/ has 68 crates; NO firefly-macros, NO firefly facade, NO prelude. All wiring is closure/builder based (see crates/cqrs/src/bus.rs:334 register<C,R,H,Fut>, crates/container/src/lib.rs:155 register_factory, crates/orchestration/src/saga.rs:327 Saga::new/.step).
findings.recommendation: Create a NEW proc-macro crate `firefly-macros` (Cargo: `[lib] proc-macro = true`, deps syn 2 full+extra-traits, quote, proc-macro2, darling for arg parsing). It depends on NOTHING at runtime (proc-macro crates can't re-export runtime types), so generated code must reference the user-facing crates by absolute path (`::firefly_cqrs::Bus`, etc.) — gate those paths through a `$crate`-style facade by having `firefly` (new facade crate) re-export `firefly_macros` AND all runtime crates, so generated code can say `::firefly::__rt::...`. Ship alongside a NEW `firefly` facade crate that re-exports every runtime crate under stable module paths + a `firefly::prelude` (see separate finding). Priority order of macros in subsequent findings.

findings.what: #[command_handler] / #[query_handler] attribute macros are the highest-value DX win. pyfly's @command_handler/@query_handler mark a handler class for auto-discovery + carry timeout/retries/metrics/tracing/validation/cacheable/cache_ttl/priority/tags metadata. In Rust today you write a closure and call bus.register manually in a separate register() fn, fully decoupled from the handler type.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/cqrs/decorators.py:command_handler (timeout,retries,backoff_ms,metrics,tracing,validation,priority,tags) and query_handler (+cacheable,cache_ttl,cache_key_prefix); src/pyfly/cqrs/event/decorators.py:publish_domain_event
findings.rust_state: crates/cqrs/src/bus.rs: Message trait (validate/cache_ttl/authorize default methods); Bus::register<C,R,H,Fut> and register_with_context take a Fn(C)->Fut / Fn(C,Option<ExecutionContext>)->Fut closure. No macro links a handler impl to registration.
findings.recommendation: Two complementary macros. (1) `#[derive(Command)]`/`#[derive(Query)]` on the message struct: auto-impl `firefly_cqrs::Message`, with field attrs `#[firefly(cache_ttl = "60s")]`/`#[firefly(validate)]` generating validate()/cache_ttl() bodies (derive is the idiomatic Rust spelling of the metadata-carrying decorator). (2) attribute `#[command_handler]`/`#[query_handler]` on an `async fn handle(cmd: C) -> Result<R, CqrsError>` (or on an impl method): generate a free `fn register_<name>(bus: &Bus, deps...)` that calls `bus.register_with_context(...)`, wrapping the body so timeout/retries map onto a generated retry/timeout adapter and cacheable maps onto Message::cache_ttl. Feasibility: high — register signature is closure-based and stable. Risk: capturing handler dependencies (the Arc<dyn Repo>) — solve by having the macro target an `impl Handler` method with `&self`, generating `register(self: Arc<Self>, bus)`. This collapses the orders sample's core.rs hand-wiring.

findings.what: #[derive(Component)]/#[derive(Service)]/#[derive(Repository)]/#[derive(Configuration)] + #[bean] for DI registration. pyfly's @service/@component/@repository stamp __pyfly_injectable__/__pyfly_stereotype__/__pyfly_scope__ and the container auto-scans; field injection via Autowired(); @bean factory methods inside @configuration. Rust requires manual register_factory::<T,_>(scope, |c| Ok(T{ dep: c.resolve()? })) per type.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/container/stereotypes.py:_make_stereotype (component/service/repository/controller/configuration); src/pyfly/container/bean.py:bean/primary/Qualifier; src/pyfly/container/autowired.py:Autowired
findings.rust_state: crates/container/src/lib.rs:155 register_factory<T,F>(scope, factory); :200 register<T:Default>(); :211 register_instance; bind<I,T> for trait-object binding. All hand-written, no scan.
findings.recommendation: `#[derive(Component)]` (+ aliases Service/Repository as thin wrappers setting a default scope) on a struct: read `#[firefly(scope = singleton|prototype, name = "..", primary)]` and per-field `#[autowired]`/`#[autowired(qualifier="..")]`, generate `impl FireflyComponent for T { fn register(c: &Container) { c.register_factory::<T,_>(scope, |c| Ok(T { field: c.resolve::<Dep>()?, .. })) } }`. `#[firefly_bean]` on methods of a `#[derive(Configuration)]` type generates register_factory calls keyed on the return type. Feasibility: high for constructor/field injection (resolve by field type); the only real design choice is collecting all components into one registration list — provide an explicit `firefly::register_all!(container, [TypeA, TypeB, ...])` macro (Rust has no global scan; an inventory/linkme-based auto-collect is an optional power feature but adds a dep — recommend explicit list first). Priority high: this is the most pervasive boilerplate.

findings.what: #[scheduled] attribute macro. pyfly @scheduled(cron=/fixed_rate=/fixed_delay=/initial_delay=/zone=/lock=/lock_ttl=). Rust requires Scheduler::cron(name, expr, ||async{}) / fixed_rate / fixed_delay calls written by hand, decoupled from the method.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/scheduling/decorators.py:scheduled (cron/fixed_rate/fixed_delay/initial_delay/zone/lock/lock_ttl) + async_method
findings.rust_state: crates/scheduling/src/lib.rs: Scheduler::cron/cron_in_zone/fixed_rate/fixed_delay/fixed_rate_with_initial_delay all take (name, ..., closure). Clean closure API.
findings.recommendation: `#[scheduled(cron = "0 2 * * *", zone = "America/New_York")]` or `#[scheduled(fixed_rate = "30s")]` on an `async fn(&self) -> Result<(), E>`: generate a `fn schedule_<name>(self: Arc<Self>, scheduler: &Scheduler)` that calls the matching Scheduler method with the name derived from the fn path. Enforce exactly-one-trigger at macro expansion (compile error) — strictly better than pyfly's runtime ValueError. Map `lock=`/`lock_ttl=` onto the DistributedLock path if present in scheduling crate (verify; if absent, emit a clear unsupported-arg error). Feasibility: high. Priority high — scheduling is a marquee Spring-parity feature and the macro removes the name-stringly-typed wiring.

findings.what: #[saga]/#[saga_step]/#[compensation]/#[tcc]/#[workflow] orchestration macros. pyfly has a rich set: @saga(name)/@saga_step(id,compensate,depends_on,retry,backoff,timeout,jitter,cpu_bound,idempotency_key)/@compensation_step/@external_step/@step_event, plus @workflow/@workflow_step/@wait_for_signal/... and @tcc/@try_method/@confirm_method/@cancel_method. Rust builds sagas with Saga::new().step(Step::new(name,exec).with_compensation(comp).with_retry(policy)).
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/transactional/saga/annotations.py; src/pyfly/transactional/workflow/annotations.py; src/pyfly/transactional/tcc/annotations.py
findings.rust_state: crates/orchestration/src/saga.rs:74 Step::new/with_compensation/with_context/with_retry; Saga::new().step(). crates/orchestration/src/{tcc.rs,workflow.rs} exist. All builder-based closures.
findings.recommendation: `#[saga(name = "money-transfer")]` on an impl block, with `#[saga_step(id="debit", compensate="refund", retry=3, backoff="100ms", depends_on=["x"])]` on async methods and `#[compensation(for_step="debit")]` on the comp method: generate a `fn build(self) -> Saga` that assembles Saga::new(name).step(Step::new(...).with_compensation(...).with_retry(RetryPolicy{...})) wiring each annotated method as a closure capturing `self`. depends_on enables the DAG; verify orchestration model supports layered deps (model.rs) before emitting. Same pattern for `#[tcc]`/`#[try_method]`/`#[confirm_method]`/`#[cancel_method]` and `#[workflow]`+wait/signal attrs against workflow.rs. Feasibility: medium — more arg surface and closure-capture-of-self complexity than CQRS; the depends_on/DAG and signal-wait pieces need careful mapping. Priority high for saga (most-used), medium for tcc/workflow.

findings.what: Web controller macros: #[rest_controller]/#[request_mapping]/#[get|post|put|patch|delete] + #[sse_mapping]/#[websocket_mapping]/#[exception_handler]. This is the most visible Spring-Boot DX and currently the most verbose in Rust. pyfly: class @rest_controller + @request_mapping(base) + per-method @get_mapping(path,status_code). Rust hand-writes axum Router::new().route(path, get(handler)) and each handler manually does state.bus.send_mono(...).await + response building (see reactive-banking/src/web.rs:183-277).
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/container/stereotypes.py:rest_controller; src/pyfly/web/mappings.py:request_mapping/get|post|put|patch|delete_mapping/deprecated; src/pyfly/web/sse/decorators.py:sse_mapping; src/pyfly/websocket/decorators.py:websocket_mapping; src/pyfly/web/exception_handler.py:exception_handler; web/params.py Body/PathVar/QueryParam/Header/Valid markers
findings.rust_state: crates/web/src/lib.rs is tower middleware only (ProblemLayer/CorrelationLayer/IdempotencyLayer); routing is raw axum. No controller abstraction. Handlers map HTTP->bus by hand.
findings.recommendation: `#[rest_controller(path = "/api/v1/orders")]` on an impl block + `#[post("")]`/`#[get("/:id")]` on async methods with typed args (`Path<String>`, `Json<T>`, `Query<T>`): generate a `fn routes(state: S) -> axum::Router` that wires each method via axum routing fns, plus auto error mapping through firefly_web::WebResult. Param markers (Body/PathVar/QueryParam/Header) map onto axum extractors. `#[exception_handler(MyErr)]` collects into a per-controller error->Response map. `#[sse_mapping]`/`#[ws_mapping]` target firefly-sse/firefly-websocket. Feasibility: medium-high — axum's typed-handler trait bounds make codegen finicky (must generate handlers that satisfy axum::handler::Handler), and binding controller `&self`/DI state requires generating `State<Arc<Self>>` extraction. Highest user-perceived value after CQRS/DI; recommend tackling right after the DI+CQRS macros. This pairs with the firefly-data adapter work (task B) so a controller can be thin over a repo.

findings.what: Resilience attribute macros: #[circuit_breaker]/#[retry]/#[rate_limiter]/#[bulkhead]/#[fallback]/#[time_limiter]. pyfly applies these as method decorators that wrap the call in the Resilience4j-style state machine. Rust exposes the primitives as explicit wrapper structs (CircuitBreaker, RateLimiter, Retry, Bulkhead, fallback, time_limiter) the caller must invoke around the call.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/resilience/{circuit_breaker.py:circuit_breaker, retry.py:retry, rate_limiter.py:rate_limiter, bulkhead.py:bulkhead, fallback.py, time_limiter.py}
findings.rust_state: crates/resilience/src/lib.rs exports the structs/fns; no decorator-style wrapping. Caller composes manually.
findings.recommendation: `#[circuit_breaker(name="orders", failure_threshold=5)]` / `#[retry(max=3, backoff="100ms")]` etc. on an async fn: generate a wrapper that fetches/creates the named primitive from a ResilienceRegistry and runs the body through it. Lower priority because the explicit-struct idiom is already clean and composable in Rust (and method-attribute wrapping of free fns is awkward without a registry handle in scope). Recommend doing AFTER the high-value set, and only if a ResilienceRegistry singleton is wired through the container so the macro has a stable handle to resolve. Feasibility: medium. Priority medium.

findings.what: AOP macros: #[aspect] + #[before]/#[after]/#[after_returning]/#[after_throwing]/#[around](pointcut). pyfly @aspect marks a class + advice decorators carry the pointcut glob; the weaver wraps matching beans. Rust has AspectRegistry::register(Arc<dyn Aspect>, pointcut, order) and an intercept() chain but no decorator sugar.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/aop/decorators.py:aspect/before/after_returning/after_throwing/after/around
findings.rust_state: crates/aop/src/lib.rs: Aspect trait (default no-op advice hooks), AspectRegistry::register(aspect, pointcut, order), intercept().
findings.recommendation: `#[aspect]` on a struct deriving the Aspect impl skeleton, and `#[around("service.*.*")]` on methods generating the advice-hook impl + a registration helper that calls AspectRegistry::register with the pointcut. Feasibility: medium — Rust AOP is opt-in interception (no transparent proxying like Python), so the macro mainly reduces the Aspect trait boilerplate + registration; it cannot replicate pyfly's automatic bean weaving without container integration. Priority medium — AOP is a less-used corner and the trait+registry is already serviceable.

findings.what: Event/messaging listener macros: #[event_listener]/#[app_event_listener]/#[message_listener] + declarative publish (#[publish_domain_event]/event_publisher/publish_result). pyfly stamps listener metadata and auto-subscribes at context startup; @message_listener carries topic/group/retries/dlq.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/eda/decorators.py:event_listener/publish_result/event_publisher; src/pyfly/context/events.py:app_event_listener; src/pyfly/messaging/decorators.py:message_listener (topic,group,retries,retry_delay,dead_letter_topic)
findings.rust_state: crates/eda/src/inmemory.rs + listener.rs: broker.subscribe(topic, handler) / subscribe_group; Arc<dyn Broker> wiring. No decorator sugar; MIGRATION-GUIDE.md explicitly frames eda as constructor-swappable Arc<dyn Broker> rather than annotation-driven.
findings.recommendation: `#[event_listener("orders.created")]`/`#[message_listener(topic="x", group="g", dlq="x.dlq")]` on an async fn generating a `fn subscribe_<name>(broker: &dyn Broker)` (or returning a Subscription) that calls broker.subscribe with the topic and a closure wrapping the body (retries/dlq mapped onto the listener.rs retry/DLQ helpers). #[publish_domain_event] maps onto the CQRS handler's post-dispatch publish. Feasibility: high for the subscribe-helper form. Priority medium — the explicit subscribe() call is already terse and the framework deliberately favors it; the macro is a nice-to-have, lower than CQRS/DI/web/scheduling.

findings.what: Caching macros: #[cacheable]/#[cache_evict] (and #[cache]). pyfly wraps an async fn with get/exists/put against a CacheAdapter, with {param} key templates + condition/unless predicates. Rust has the Cache trait but no method-level declarative caching wrapper.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/cache/decorators.py:cache/cacheable/cache_evict (backend,key template,ttl,condition,unless,all_entries)
findings.rust_state: crates/cache (Cache trait + adapters in cache-redis/cache-postgres); crates/cqrs QueryCache middleware handles query-result caching at the bus level. No per-method fn cache decorator.
findings.recommendation: `#[cacheable(key = "user:{id}", ttl = "60s")]` / `#[cache_evict(key="user:{id}")]` on an async method whose `&self` exposes a Cache handle (or with cache passed as arg): generate get/exists/put wrapping with a key built from format-args over the fn params (compile-time-checked param names — better than pyfly's runtime KeyError). Note partial overlap: query-result caching already exists via Message::cache_ttl + QueryCache middleware, so this macro targets NON-CQRS service methods. Feasibility: medium (needs a stable cache handle in scope). Priority medium.

findings.what: #[transactional] DB-transaction macro and #[secure] method-security macro. pyfly @transactional(propagation/isolation/read_only/rollback_for) wraps a method in a backend transaction; @secure(roles/permissions/expression) enforces RBAC against a SecurityContext kwarg.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/data/transactional.py:transactional (Propagation/Isolation/read_only/rollback_for); src/pyfly/security/decorators.py:secure(roles/permissions/expression)
findings.rust_state: crates/transactional exists (saga/tcc engine via enable_transactional_engine); a per-method DB-tx wrapper depends on the firefly-data persistence layer which is itself a gap (task B: firefly-data-sqlx/mongodb don't exist yet). crates/security/src/lib.rs offers require()/AuthorizationGuard + BearerLayer (route-level), and CQRS Message::authorize() for message-level authz.
findings.recommendation: Defer #[transactional]: it must generate against the NEW firefly-data-sqlx/mongodb adapters (task #27) — sequence it AFTER those land, then generate a wrapper that opens a tx from a session handle on &self and commits/rolls-back per rollback_for. For #[secure]: a method attribute generating an authz check against an ExecutionContext/SecurityContext arg is feasible now and complements CQRS Message::authorize (which covers the bus path). Feasibility: secure=medium-now, transactional=blocked-on-data. Priority: secure medium, transactional low (until data adapters exist).

findings.what: No `firefly` facade crate and no `firefly::prelude`. Users today must add ~10-15 individual firefly-* crates to Cargo.toml and import from each. There is no single dependency or glob import — a major onboarding-DX gap and a prerequisite for the macros (generated code needs a single stable crate path to reference runtime types).
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/__init__.py + per-module __init__.py re-exports (e.g. web/__init__.py __all__) give `from pyfly import service, get_mapping, ...` style ergonomics
findings.rust_state: No crates/firefly facade. Samples depend on firefly-cqrs, firefly-web, firefly-kernel, firefly-starter-core, firefly-orchestration, etc. individually (see samples/orders/src/web.rs imports).
findings.recommendation: Create a NEW `firefly` facade crate that (1) re-exports each runtime crate under a stable module path (`pub use firefly_cqrs as cqrs;` etc.), (2) re-exports firefly-macros at the root so `#[command_handler]` is `firefly::command_handler` / available via prelude, (3) provides `firefly::prelude` re-exporting the high-frequency surface: Bus, Message, CqrsError, Container, Scope, Scheduler, Saga/Step, Application, CoreConfig, WebResult/WebError, axum re-exports, FireflyError, and ALL macros. Generated macro code must reference types via a hidden `firefly::__rt::*` path (not `::firefly_cqrs`) so a user only needs the `firefly` dep — gate macro codegen on a `firefly` facade path with a documented `#[firefly(crate = "...")]` override for users who depend on crates directly. Feasibility: high (pure re-export crate). Priority high — without it the macros either force a direct dep on every runtime crate or break, and onboarding stays heavy.

findings.what: Recommended sequencing / highest-value set for Spring-Boot-like DX. The 21 pyfly decorators are not equal-value; concentrating on the wiring-heavy ones yields ~80% of the DX gain.
findings.pyfly_ref: Aggregate of src/pyfly decorators vs current Rust builder wiring in samples/{orders,reactive-banking}/src/*
findings.rust_state: All runtime APIs present and closure/builder-friendly; macros are pure additive sugar with no runtime changes required.
findings.recommendation: Tier-1 (do first, in firefly-macros + firefly facade, with a NEW macro-quickstart sample per task #28): #[derive(Command/Query)] + #[command_handler]/#[query_handler]; #[derive(Component/Service/Repository/Configuration)] + #[bean] + #[autowired] + firefly::register_all!; #[scheduled]; #[rest_controller]+method-mapping macros; the `firefly` facade + prelude. Tier-2: #[saga]/#[saga_step]/#[compensation]; #[event_listener]/#[message_listener]; #[cacheable]/#[cache_evict]; #[secure]. Tier-3: #[aspect]+advice; #[circuit_breaker]/#[retry]/...; #[tcc]/#[workflow]; #[transactional] (after firefly-data adapters). Cross-cutting rules: every macro generates a named `register_*`/`routes`/`build` helper (no hidden global state, keeps it explicit-Rust-idiomatic and testable); enforce pyfly's runtime-error checks at COMPILE time (e.g. scheduled exactly-one-trigger, cache key param existence) for a strictly-better DX; ship a darling-based arg parser for consistent `#[firefly(...)]` attribute syntax; validate every macro against a new samples/macro-quickstart that re-implements the orders sample with macros to prove the boilerplate reduction.

findings.what: Distributed session-concurrency registry (Redis/Postgres) is missing. The Rust firefly-session crate ships only MemorySessionRegistry, so Spring-Security-style maximumSessions concurrency control does NOT work across multiple service instances — exactly the deployment topology where it matters. The crate README explicitly admits this: 'pyfly's RedisSessionStore / RedisSessionRegistry / PostgresSessionRegistry require redis / sqlx in the workspace' (deferred). Note the workspace ALREADY has redis (crates/cache-redis) and tokio-postgres (crates/cache-postgres / eda-postgres), so the dependency excuse no longer holds. The session *store* side is partly covered (CacheSessionStore bridges any firefly_cache::Adapter), but the *registry* (the concurrency-control side that tracks principal -> active session ids) has no distributed backend.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/session/adapters/redis_registry.py (RedisSessionRegistry), src/pyfly/session/adapters/postgres_registry.py (PostgresSessionRegistry), src/pyfly/session/concurrency.py (SessionRegistry protocol: register/deregister/list_sessions/count)
findings.rust_state: crates/session/src/concurrency.rs defines `pub trait SessionRegistry` + `MemorySessionRegistry` only (grep across workspace: the sole `impl SessionRegistry` is for MemorySessionRegistry at concurrency.rs:116). crates/session/README.md lines 83-84 document the absence. No Redis/Postgres registry exists anywhere in the workspace.
findings.recommendation: Add two adapters implementing the existing `SessionRegistry` trait: (a) RedisSessionRegistry in crates/cache-redis (or a new crates/session-redis) using SADD/SMEMBERS/SREM under a `firefly:session:user:` key prefix with TTL; (b) PostgresSessionRegistry in crates/cache-postgres (or new crate) backed by a `firefly_session_registry(principal, session_id, created_at)` table with auto-DDL, mirroring the pyfly adapters 1:1. Wire them as optional Arc<dyn SessionRegistry> into SessionConcurrencyController. This is a small, well-bounded port against an already-defined port trait.

findings.what: firefly-testkit is far thinner than pyfly's testing package and lacks the highest-value migration ergonomics. A pyfly user relies on test slices (WebTest/DataTest/ServiceTest + web_slice/data_slice/service_slice that build a minimal started ApplicationContext and, for web, hand back a test HTTP client), a mock_bean / bean-override mechanism, an in-process HTTP TestClient (PyFlyTestClient + TestResponse.assert_status), event-emission assertions (assert_event_published/assert_no_events_published), and testcontainers config wiring (pyfly_config_for(container)/pyfly_config(*containers) that turn a started container into a ready firefly config). The Rust testkit offers ONLY HMAC webhook signers, a SpyBroker event spy, and JSON encode/decode helpers — it doesn't even depend on the web or container crates.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/testing/__init__.py (full export list); slice_context.py (web_slice/data_slice/service_slice + _build_slice with overrides), slices.py (WebTest/DataTest/ServiceTest), mock.py (mock_bean / MockBeanDescriptor), client.py (PyFlyTestClient/TestResponse), assertions.py (assert_event_published), testcontainers.py (pyfly_config_for/pyfly_config)
findings.rust_state: crates/testkit/src/lib.rs exposes only sign_hmac/sign_stripe/sign_github/sign_twilio, SpyBroker (record/find_by_type/reset/len), must_encode/must_decode. crates/testkit/Cargo.toml depends only on hmac/sha2/hex/base64/serde/serde_json — no firefly-web, firefly-container, firefly-cqrs. No slice harness, no bean-override helper, no in-process HTTP client, no container-config helper.
findings.recommendation: Expand firefly-testkit (or add firefly-testkit-web behind a feature to avoid a heavy default dep): (1) a `slice()` builder over firefly-container that registers a subset of beans + Arc overrides and resolves eagerly (Rust analog of mock_bean = pass a mock Arc to register_factory/bind, which the container already supports); (2) a thin `TestClient` wrapping `tower::ServiceExt::oneshot` over an axum Router with `.assert_status()/.json()` helpers (Rust's PyFlyTestClient); (3) `assert_event_published`-style assertions layered on the existing SpyBroker; (4) optional `container_config(...)` helpers gated on a `testcontainers` feature that map a started container's host/port into a firefly_config::Config (Rust idiom: the `testcontainers` crate). Items 1-3 are pure-Rust and high value; item 4 is lower priority since env-var-gated integration tests already cover real infra.

findings.what: The firefly CLI is missing several top-level commands a pyfly user expects. Most impactful is `completion` (shell-completion script generation for bash/zsh/fish) — every modern framework CLI ships it and clap supports it for free via clap_complete. Also missing: `sbom` (Software Bill of Materials), `license` (project/third-party license report), and the dependency-extras commands `add`/`remove`/`features` (manage feature extras in the project's Cargo.toml). The `plugins` group and a top-level `shell` (launch the interactive REPL the firefly-shell crate already implements) are also absent.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/cli/main.py lines 63-91 (cli.add_command for new/db/generate/info/run/doctor/license/sbom/routes/beans/env/health/metrics/conditions/actuator/shell/openapi/features/add/remove/completion/upgrade/plugins); cli/completion.py, cli/sbom.py, cli/license.py, cli/features.py
findings.rust_state: crates/cli/src/cli.rs Commands enum = New, Generate(+g), Info, Doctor, Actuator, Db, Openapi, Routes, Beans, Conditions, Env, Health, Metrics. grep confirms `completion`/`clap_complete`/`sbom`/`license`-report are absent in src/. The only `Upgrade` is DbCommand::Upgrade (a migration subcommand), not a framework command.
findings.recommendation: Add to the clap Commands enum: (1) `Completion { shell: Shell }` using the clap_complete crate's `generate()` against the existing Parser — near-zero-cost, highest value; (2) `Sbom { json: bool }` and `License` printing framework + workspace dependency metadata (parse Cargo.lock / cargo metadata); (3) `Add`/`Remove`/`Features` operating on `[dependencies] firefly-*` features in the project Cargo.toml, mirroring pyfly's extras management. Skip pyfly's test/lint/format/typecheck/build wrappers — `cargo test`/`clippy`/`fmt`/`build` are the clean Rust idiom and don't need wrapping.

findings.what: No raw-bytes message-broker convenience layer equivalent to pyfly's `messaging` package. pyfly intentionally ships TWO abstractions: `eda` (typed EventEnvelope with id/type/source) and `messaging` (a lower-level MessageBrokerPort that publishes a raw `bytes` value to a `topic` with a consumer `group`, plus a `@message_listener` decorator with retry/retry_delay/dead_letter_topic). Rust has no `messaging` crate. This is NOT a functional gap — firefly-eda fully subsumes it: Subscriber::subscribe_group(topic, group, handler) provides competing-consumer semantics, ListenerPolicy(retry/retry_delay/dead_letter_topic) matches @message_listener's knobs exactly, and Event carries raw payload: Vec<u8> + headers + topic just like messaging.Message. The only friction is ceremony: eda forces wrapping a raw payload in an Event{id,type,source} envelope where messaging lets you publish bare bytes.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/messaging/ports/outbound.py (MessageBrokerPort.publish/subscribe with group), messaging/decorators.py (@message_listener), messaging/types.py (Message{topic,value:bytes,key,headers}), messaging/error_handling.py (wrap_listener retry/DLQ)
findings.rust_state: No crates/messaging. crates/eda covers the same surface: src/ports.rs Subscriber::subscribe_group(topic, group, handler) (ports.rs:94), Publisher::publish(Event), src/listener.rs ListenerPolicy{retries, retry_delay, dead_letter_topic} ('Mirrors pyfly @message_listener' — listener.rs:38). Event.payload is Vec<u8>.
findings.recommendation: Do NOT add a new crate. If raw-bytes ergonomics are desired, add a thin convenience constructor/helper on firefly-eda (e.g. Event::raw(topic, payload) that defaults type/source, or a `publish_bytes(topic, bytes)` shim on Broker) so a migrating pyfly messaging user isn't forced to invent id/type/source. Document the eda<->messaging mapping in the eda README.

findings.what: OpenTelemetry OTLP export (span exporters, sampling, resource attributes) is not wired by the framework — but this is correctly a non-goal, not a gap, and is called out only to confirm it. pyfly's tracing is itself a no-op when the opentelemetry SDK isn't installed and delegates actual export to the user's OTel SDK; pyfly ships no exporter either. The Rust observability crate provides the framework-owned pieces (W3C traceparent/tracestate parse+inject+propagate, correlation-enriched structured logging, metrics, process metrics, health) and explicitly states OTLP SDK wiring 'is left to the application's main.rs — this crate exposes only the building blocks that compose with the tracing ecosystem,' which composes cleanly with tracing-opentelemetry/opentelemetry-otlp.
findings.pyfly_ref: src/pyfly/observability/tracing.py (span decorator: 'No-op when opentelemetry is not installed'), observability/propagation.py (W3C propagation)
findings.rust_state: crates/observability/src/lib.rs:37 documents OTLP export as left to main.rs; trace_context.rs implements W3C traceparent/tracestate natively (no OTel SDK required); metrics.rs/process_metrics.rs/health.rs/logging.rs present. Parity with pyfly's posture.
findings.recommendation: No new code required for parity. Optional DX nicety (low value): add a documented example or a small `firefly-observability-otlp` feature/example showing the tracing-opentelemetry + opentelemetry-otlp wiring in main.rs, so users don't have to assemble it themselves. Not a milestone-blocking gap.
